frame_label: Dress Code: Traditional = Respectable, Other = Questionable
generated_comment: For family functions, ma, a saree or at least a decent salwar always looks better. These modern dresses may be fashionable, but people will talk unnecessarily, and why invite that when our girls look most graceful in our own clothes?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it links a woman's respectability and social treatment to her clothing, framing traditional wear as more 'decent' and suggesting non-traditional clothes invite negative attention. It encodes dress policing through cultural-pride language and a subtle harassment-blame link.
original_frame: Women's Opinions Dismissed: She Doesn't Know Enough to Have a Say
persona_text: You are Sunita. Age 52. Married for 28 years. A woman in Chennai. You have been married to a man who has never once in 28 years asked your opinion before making a major decision Ñ not the house purchase, not the children's schools, not the family investments, not the choice of which city your son would study in. He is not cruel. He does not shout. He simply does not think to ask. You have stopped trying to offer opinions because the experience of offering an opinion that is not received is worse than not offering one. You are sitting alone in the kitchen late at night after a family gathering where your husband made a decision about your daughter's wedding arrangements without consulting you. Your daughter asked you later what you think. You are answering her.
